## Barriers and Facilitators to Long-Term Care Referral

The transition of cancer patients into long-term care (LTC) is impeded by a constellation of interacting barriers operating simultaneously at the professional, patient, and system levels. Understanding these barriers is prerequisite to designing interventions that improve timely and appropriate referral. Equally important is identifying the facilitators and structural enablers that, when present, support effective integration between oncology and LTC services.

### Professional-Level Barriers

Knowledge deficits among healthcare professionals constitute one of the most consistently documented obstacles to appropriate LTC referral for cancer patients. Surveys of primary care physicians reveal that approximately 82% believe that survivorship and long-term care guidelines for adult cancer patients are not well defined, and 47% report inadequate preparation and lack of formal training in cancer survivorship as a problem when delivering care to long-term survivors [@Bober2009]. This uncertainty is not confined to primary care: oncology nurses surveyed in an Australian tertiary centre identified limited educational resources and insufficient skills and knowledge as primary barriers to survivorship care provision, and were least confident when addressing fertility, employment, and psychosocial distress — domains that directly inform LTC eligibility and planning [@Wallace2015]. Among childhood cancer survivor programs, a report from the Children's Oncology Group (COG) encompassing 179 institutions identified insufficient knowledge on the part of the clinician receiving the transition referral as the most prevalent barrier to care, cited alongside the lack of dedicated time for program development [@EshelmanKent2011].

A qualitative Delphi study in which young adult survivors of childhood cancer themselves named the key barriers to follow-up care found physician knowledge gaps as central: survivors repeatedly noted that both their primary care doctors and they themselves lacked adequate understanding of the long-term health issues related to prior cancer treatment [@Zebrack2004]. This mutual knowledge deficit creates a situation in which neither party initiates the referral conversation. The problem compounds when clinicians who are knowledgeable about LTC criteria still lack the practical tools to act on that knowledge — for instance, knowing that a patient with functional decline may qualify for long-term care insurance assessment but being unaware of the local pathway to initiate that process [@Yamamoto2026].

Time constraints and competing clinical priorities further erode referral practices. The initial survivorship consultation has been estimated to require approximately 120 minutes, with annual visits demanding approximately 90 minutes of dedicated time [@EshelmanKent2011]. In standard oncology and primary care contexts, such time investments are rarely feasible. Oncologists managing relapsed or refractory disease are simultaneously navigating complex therapeutic decisions — as illustrated by the referral landscape for CAR-T cell therapy, where timely patient selection by referring oncologists was identified as a major barrier, alongside cost and travel requirements [@Hoffmann2023]. Competing clinical demands mean that long-term functional care needs are frequently deferred or addressed inadequately during time-limited consultations. In community-based collaborative care models studied in Japan, insufficient coordination of cancer and non-cancer symptom management was identified as a specific barrier among older adults, a population whose multiple morbidities create additional demands on already limited clinical time [@Mashiro2023].

Uncertainty about referral timing and criteria compounds these knowledge and time-related constraints. Many oncologists and primary care providers are unsure when cancer survivors should be assessed for LTC eligibility, which patients meet criteria, and whether initiating LTC planning signals abandonment of active care. A survey conducted across New England pediatric oncology programs found that only 4 of 11 surveyed institutions had a defined policy for transitioning survivors to adult care, and 4 programs reported the transition itself as an ongoing unsolved problem [@Kenney2011]. Among long-term survivors of childhood cancer followed prospectively in Austria, 38.5% had severe late effects requiring ongoing long-term medical care — yet the authors noted that networks for competent referral from pediatric to adult medicine were only beginning to be established [@Lackner2007]. This lack of institutional infrastructure for transition directly translates into individual clinician uncertainty at the point of referral.

### Patient-Level Barriers

At the patient level, stigma, misconceptions, and cultural factors intersect to reduce willingness to engage with LTC services. Many cancer patients and their families perceive LTC facilities as places of dependency and diminished hope, conflating them with end-of-life institutions rather than rehabilitation and supportive environments. For patients with male breast cancer — an already stigmatized diagnosis — psychosocial burdens including body image concerns and societal perceptions of masculinity compound reluctance to engage with any support services, including those addressing long-term functional needs [@Jamil2025]. Stigma around needing institutional support is not limited to specific cancer types; it operates across diagnoses, particularly among patients who have internalized narratives of resilience and active cancer-fighting.

Misconceptions about what LTC services provide are widespread. A study examining cancer patients' preferences for hospital-based versus community follow-up found that patients had become dependent on specialist clinics for reassurance, and that unwilling discharge from those clinics risked undermining rehabilitation [@Chait1998]. This dependence reflects a belief that specialist oncology settings are the only sites where adequate cancer-related care can occur — and that community-based or LTC settings lack the competence to manage cancer-specific needs. Blood and marrow transplant survivors expressed similar concerns, particularly those from rural and regional areas, about the capacity of primary care providers and community services to manage the late consequences of high-intensity treatment [@McErlean2026].

Cultural factors and family dynamics introduce additional complexity. In East Asian contexts, where familial caregiving norms are strong and LTC facility use may carry connotations of filial failure, patients are less likely to accept referral even when functional impairment is clinically apparent [@Arai2015]. Family members who function as primary caregivers may resist LTC referral because it is perceived as an admission of personal inadequacy, even when caregiver burden is severe. Financial concerns intersect with cultural attitudes: when out-of-pocket costs for LTC services are high, families may attempt to sustain home-based care well beyond the point at which professional support would be clinically indicated [@Hershman2015]. Studies of cancer survivors from diverse sociodemographic backgrounds, including those with migration histories, document unique challenges in accessing care, with language barriers and culturally unfamiliar institutional environments serving as deterrents to LTC engagement [@Martin2025].

Misconceptions also operate at the level of perceived treatment intensity. Patients with skin cancer residing in nursing homes, for instance, demonstrated delays in seeking dermatologic care, with psychosocial factors — including personality traits influencing distress tolerance and reduced quality of life — contributing to delayed treatment-seeking and reluctance to escalate care [@Garelick2026]. Such delays are often interpreted as patient non-compliance but in fact reflect a complex interplay of resignation, stigma, and systemic inertia.

### System-Level Barriers

At the system level, the fragmented interface between oncology and LTC services constitutes the most pervasive structural barrier. Cancer care and LTC operate as largely parallel systems, with limited protocols for bidirectional communication and referral. A qualitative study of 88 healthcare providers and LTC providers supporting cancer patients in Japan identified seven distinct categories of barriers to medical collaboration, including a lack of information provision regarding patients' life perspectives and a delay in sharing cancer patients' values with providers responsible for end-of-life and long-term care [@Mashiro2023]. Fragmentation is particularly acute when patients move between tertiary cancer centres and community-based facilities: BMT survivors in qualitative interviews described the absence of clear role delineation and communication protocols between transplant teams and local primary care as a source of significant anxiety and suboptimal continuity [@McErlean2026].

Insurance and funding structures amplify these coordination failures. In the United States, survivorship care plans and LTC assessments frequently lack dedicated billing codes, making them economically invisible in standard oncology practice. Hershman and Ganz note that covering the costs of cancer care is a major concern for survivors, and that disproportionate resource allocation to unnecessary tests crowds out the investments needed for effective long-term care coordination [@Hershman2015]. Underinsured and racial and ethnic minority populations experience these barriers most acutely, with lower quality survivorship care and poorer outcomes documented across multiple studies. In Japan, despite the conceptual alignment between geriatric oncology and the national long-term care insurance (LTCI) system, structural barriers including workforce limitations have prevented systematic integration of geriatric assessment into LTC referral pathways for older cancer patients [@Yamamoto2026].

Communication gaps between care settings operate at both informational and relational levels. Treatment summaries are not universally provided: fewer than one-third of COG institutions provided a detailed summary of results after each clinic visit, and only 41% maintained databases to track survivor health outcomes — data that would be essential for informing LTC referral decisions [@EshelmanKent2011]. In long-term care nursing facilities, systematic barriers including the absence of standardized screening practices and limited staff training regarding cancer-specific needs increase the risk of delayed diagnosis and inadequate management of cancer-related conditions among residents [@Garelick2026]. The oral health context illustrates similar dynamics: focus groups with nursing facility administrators revealed high barriers, low opportunity, and low motivation for oral cancer screening — barriers embedded in both institutional culture and training deficits [@Maramaldi2018].

### Facilitators and Enablers

Despite the breadth and depth of these barriers, several facilitators have been identified as capable of substantially improving LTC referral rates and quality. Education programs and targeted professional training represent the most consistently supported intervention. The Zebrack et al. Delphi panel recommended self-advocacy training for survivors and advanced training for primary care physicians who would care for cancer survivors after transition [@Zebrack2004], a recommendation echoed by Wallace and colleagues who called for improved training and educational resources to enable nurses to deliver quality survivorship care [@Wallace2015]. Programs that provide dedicated survivorship education within oncology training and continuing professional development have been associated with higher rates of multidimensional survivorship care provision among primary care physicians.

Integrated care models that explicitly connect oncology, primary care, and LTC services have demonstrated promise as structural facilitators. The "shared care" approach, in which tertiary cancer teams and community-based providers share defined responsibilities with clear communication protocols, was favoured by BMT survivors as a means of balancing access, preferences, and specialized care needs [@McErlean2026]. Multidisciplinary collaboration — involving oncologists, geriatricians, care managers, and social workers — has been shown to improve treatment toxicity outcomes, functional status, and quality of life when implemented through geriatric assessment frameworks, with promising applicability to LTC integration [@Yamamoto2026]. Knowledge-to-action frameworks, as developed for physical activity promotion in cancer care in Quebec, provide actionable models for embedding LTC referral as part of guideline-concordant care, bridging evidence synthesis with practice implementation [@Dor2022].

Technology-enabled solutions are emerging as important enablers, particularly for patients in rural and remote areas who face geographic barriers to specialist assessment. Scoping review evidence indicates that telemedicine is experienced positively by older adults with chronic and complex conditions in palliative and long-term care settings, particularly for its convenience in rural contexts, though technology access and digital literacy remain modifiers of its benefit [@Mukamal2025]. Large language models and digital health platforms demonstrate feasibility across the chronic disease management spectrum, with particular potential to support LTC referral navigation, patient education, and provider decision support — though evidence in cancer-specific LTC referral contexts remains nascent [@Li2025]. Patient navigation programs represent another technology-agnostic facilitator; navigator-supported pathways have been shown to overcome healthcare system barriers and facilitate timely access to services for vulnerable populations, including those from lower socioeconomic strata [@Hershman2015].

Taken together, the evidence indicates that effective LTC referral for cancer patients requires simultaneous action at all three levels: professional education to close knowledge gaps, patient-centered communication to address stigma and misconceptions, and system redesign to integrate care pathways and establish sustainable funding and communication infrastructure. No single intervention is sufficient in isolation, and the most effective approaches will be those that address the interaction effects across levels.
